full_response:
We are asked whether CNF-SAT becomes easier if we restrict attention to
formulæ in which  

 “every clause contains at least one positive literal.”

Call this promise version POS-CNF-SAT.

Key observation  
If every clause contains a positive literal, then the truth assignment that
sets every variable to True already satisfies every clause:  
the positive literal contained in the clause is evaluated to True and the
whole clause is therefore True, regardless of what the other literals do.

Hence every such formula is satisfiable; the decision problem is therefore
trivial.

Polynomial-time algorithm  
Input: CNF formula F whose clauses all contain a positive literal.  
1.  (Optional) verify in O(|F|) time that the promise is met.  
2.  Output the assignment that sets every variable to True and answer
“SATISFIABLE”.

Correctness proof (by direct verification)  
For every clause C in F there exists a literal x that occurs positively.
Under the assignment σ with σ(v)=True for every variable v, that literal x is
True.  Therefore C is True, and since this holds for every clause, the whole
formula is True.  Hence the algorithm always outputs a satisfying
assignment, so it is correct.

Complexity  
Step 1 runs in O(|F|) time; step 2 is O(1).  Thus the total running time is
polynomial (indeed linear) in the input size.

Because the problem is solvable by such an algorithm, the friend’s alleged
polynomial-time procedure is certainly possible; there is nothing mysterious
about it.

\boxed{\text{Possible; the variant is solvable in polynomial time (in fact, always
satisfiable by setting all variables to True).}}

Confidence: 86%
